frameworks.name: deep reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: recurrent deep RL agent trained on observe-or-bet variants to learn control-sensitive policies

frameworks.name: meta-control / sense of control
frameworks.one_line: agent represents its own level of control and adapts exploration accordingly

frameworks.name: action-prediction error
frameworks.one_line: auxiliary self-supervised signal shaping internal hidden representations beyond reward

frameworks.name: exploration-exploitation trade-off
frameworks.one_line: observe-or-bet task structure forces explicit explore-vs-exploit choice each trial

core_question: How do agents (and humans) adapt their policies in response to changes in their own level of control over outcomes?
key_assumptions: agents must explicitly choose to observe or bet on each trial
internal hidden state can be shaped by auxiliary action-prediction error signals
level of control is a manipulable task variable distinct from reward
manipulations: level of control / efficacy across task settings (high vs low control)
training objective: reward-only vs reward + action-prediction-error auxiliary loss
two observe-or-bet task variants (explore-exploit balance vs control-seeking)
key_findings: reward-only agents fail to adapt policies across control settings
agents with action-prediction-error auxiliary training spend more time exploring in high-control settings
the same auxiliary-trained agents increase control-seeking behavior in low-control settings
interpretations: structuring hidden representations with action-prediction error is necessary for sensing and exploiting one's own control
credit assignment over control requires more than a scalar reward signal
limitations_noted: human behavioral comparison in the same tasks is stated as planned/ongoing rather than completed
